documentPurpose: LLM-readable project context for the ACS remake. This file summarizes the current architecture, data model, flows, milestone status, quality expectations, and future AI extension points so an AI assistant can reason about the project without relying only on prose PDFs.

project.name: ACS
project.fullName: Adventure Construction Set inspired construction kit
project.goal: Build a modern, extensible adventure construction environment inspired by 1980s ACS while keeping game rules separate from presentation so later HD 2D, 3D, richer audio, realtime modes, multi-renderer authoring choices, and AI-assisted authoring remain possible.
corePrinciples: Runtime rules and presentation assets are separate.
Editor behavior and authoring state should stay separate from editor presentation skins so future UX layers can reuse the same editing contracts.
Game content is structured data, not hardcoded behavior.
Reusable game concepts should become objects with stable ids and editor CRUD rather than loose strings.
Editor UI should follow a hierarchical design flow and show only the relevant panel for the current task.
Documentation, screenshots, PDFs, and AI-readable context should be refreshed as milestones add features.
Accepted planning or implementation changes should update roadmap.html and the related durable documentation immediately rather than waiting for a later cleanup pass.

Every milestone completion must always regenerate the human-facing PDF documents from source pages that include visible tables of contents, and replace stale screenshots with current, task-specific captures.
User Guide tutorial screenshots should show each authored step explicitly rather than relying on repeated or generic images.
New or worsened functions over cyclomatic complexity 8 should trigger refactor work. SOLID regressions should be avoided.
Every milestone completion must run the full available end-to-end quality suite with npm run quality and npm test, or explicitly document any unavailable layer before completion.
Codex collaboration history is tracked in docs/codex-chat-log.json so future LLMs can recover user decisions and current obligations.
Accepted future recommendations should be mirrored into durable pre-milestone documents instead of living only in chat.
At the start of each future milestone, summarize which remaining feature checklist items that milestone is intended to move.
Any milestone that adds or changes visible Play Mode or Edit Mode UI must update docs/ux-skinning-inventory.md and docs/ux-skinning-inventory.json.

uxSkinningInventory.status: live and must be maintained on UI-changing milestones
uxSkinningInventory.goal: Track all visible editor and runtime UI elements, shared primitives, dynamic surfaces, and skinning connection points so future skins can attach without changing function.

primaryApps.role: Browser play mode and browser editor UI.
Small API/data persistence surface for project storage.

primaryPackages.role: Shared TypeScript domain model for AdventurePackage, maps, entities, quests, assets, visual manifests, presentation settings, and starter libraries.
Raw package normalization, defaults, compatibility migration, and content shape cleanup.
Renderer-agnostic game engine for commands, movement, interactions, triggers, enemy turns, state, and events.
Canvas/runtime presentation layer, including classic ACS-inspired visual mode.
Pure editor operations for changing adventure data before browser UI wiring.
Milestone 31 boundary for AI-provider manifests, generation request/proposal contracts, provider registries, and proposal validation helpers.
Milestone 26 boundary for built-in starter library source metadata, starter snapshots, and custom library export helpers.
Milestone 30 boundary for forkable-project and standalone-playable artifact generation, runtime asset dependency collection, and publish artifact validation.
Save/load format wrappers around RuntimeSnapshot and local persistence helpers.

playMode: Classic ACS visual mode and debug grid mode render the same runtime state.
Keyboard-first movement and interaction dispatch commands into runtime-core.
Turn pacing prevents enemies from moving on every single player keypress.
Dialogue can be displayed in the classic message band while debug mode can show richer debug dialogue UI.
editorMode: Focused editor areas: Adventure, World Atlas, Map Workspace, Libraries, Logic, Test and Publish.

Current browser editor UI is one presentation shell over shared draft data and editor-core operations; future skins should preserve that separation instead of embedding authoring rules in the visual layer.
Map workspace supports brush-style tile painting, entity placement/movement, exits, and trigger markers.
Placed entity instances can have optional displayName and behaviorOverride metadata so multiple copies of one reusable definition can receive unique story names and simple local behavior roles.
Map Workspace includes a selected-cell inspector that summarizes the clicked coordinate's tile, occupant, exit, and trigger records.
Test & Publish includes a Display Rename / Reskin tool that previews and applies player-facing text changes while preserving stable ids and structured references.
Libraries hold entities, items, skills, flags, quests, traits, spells, tiles, dialogue, custom objects, and assets as organized object classes.
Libraries are moving toward a parent-child drilldown: library type, category/class, object, then full-width details below.
Map Workspace Terrain Tiles mode now populates Tile Brush options from all reusable tile definitions, then map-local palette ids, then fallback ids.

World Atlas now presents map scale as World, Region, or Local Area. Parent Region is documented as organizational metadata, not a travel restriction; exits can connect any valid source and target maps.

The classic pixel editor shows a visible paint-color swatch plus synchronized in-game, magnified, and grouping previews; the grouping preview repeats the sprite in a 4-by-4 tile block so repeated terrain seams can be judged while painting.
Editor bootstrap is queued after module initialization so table-driven rule summaries are ready before the first render.

Milestone 29A adds the testing foundation: package unit tests, headless browser editor UI smoke tests, headless browser runtime UI E2E tests, command-level runtime playtest smoke tests, and a milestone completion requirement to run npm test.

Milestone 29B adds resilient build/typecheck tooling through tools/build-workspace.mjs and default starter-library regression tests that compare non-Land classic ACS starter elements against legacy ACS/legacy_ACS_startpacks.txt.

Milestone 29C adds runtime actor action readiness evaluation through packages/runtime-core/src/actor-permissions.ts. This preflights RuntimeActionProposal permissions against ActorCapabilityProfile and ActorUsePolicy data, but does not yet execute NPC actions.

Milestone 29D adds tools/validate-docs.mjs and npm run docs:validate so guide/reference image links, required PDFs, Relay Station tutorial step screenshots, and repeated step screenshots are checked automatically.

Milestone 30A adds @acs/publishing as a pure export boundary for forkableProject and standalonePlayable artifacts, including runtime asset dependency collection, standalone authoring-data pruning, and publish artifact validation.
Milestone 30B wires release artifact export through apps/api, @acs/project-api, and the editor Test and Publish panel so immutable releases can be downloaded as forkable or standalone JSON artifacts.

Milestone 30C extends standalonePlayable exports with a generated bundle manifest, adds apps/api/src/standalone-bundle.ts, and teaches apps/web/src/index.ts to boot from ?package=... for future static standalone play bundles.

Milestone 30D adds packages/publishing/src/standalone-archive.ts so standalone bundle manifests can be packaged as ZIP archives, and the editor downloads standalone exports as .zip while the forkable path continued using the release-backed artifact flow until later packaging work.

Milestone 30E adds a release-backed standalone package preview panel in Test and Publish so designers can inspect bundle entry file, shipped file list, and runtime asset counts before downloading the standalone ZIP.
Milestone 30F adds a release readiness panel in Test and Publish that summarizes validation, release, package preview, diagnostics, and known current MVP limitations before distribution.

Milestone 30G adds publish-time release metadata so designers can enter a release label and release notes, persist them on immutable releases, and see note excerpts in release summaries and readiness checks.

Milestone 30H adds a standalone distribution manifest so release identity, entry file, content counts, and known limitations travel with each standalone package and are emitted into bundle/distribution-manifest.json.

Milestone 30I adds bundled local-launch groundwork for standalone exports, including launcher metadata plus packaged Windows helper scripts that start a tiny local static server for the exported bundle.
Milestone 30J adds packaged standalone handoff guides so exported bundles now include README.html and README.txt plus preview/readiness visibility for recommended launch instructions.
Milestone 30K adds forkable artifact preview support so editable release handoffs can be inspected in the editor alongside standalone package previews.

Milestone 30L adds a ForkableProjectManifest so editable exports carry release metadata, import guidance, handoff next steps, and known limitations, with those details surfaced in forkable preview and release readiness.
Milestone 30M adds an Artifact Comparison panel in Test and Publish so forkable and standalone exports can be compared side by side before distribution.

Milestone 30N aligns export file names and packaged release notes with the publishing manifests, adds RELEASE-NOTES.txt to standalone bundles, and surfaces the final handoff names in preview/readiness flows.

Milestone 30O packages forkable exports into ZIP handoff bundles that contain forkable-project.json, project-manifest.json, README.html, README.txt, and RELEASE-NOTES.txt, while the editor now downloads and previews the packaged forkable handoff names.
Milestone 30P adds a typed forkable package manifest with file list and entry-file data so forkable ZIP creation, validation, and preview behavior all read from one shared package description.
Milestone 30Q adds a shared Release Handoff Manifest plus a dedicated Test and Publish preview so forkable and standalone exports can be understood together from one release-level summary.
Milestone 30R adds direct Release Handoff export so that shared release-level summary can also be downloaded as its own JSON handoff document.

Milestone 30S adds a shared Artifact Integrity Report so forkable package files, standalone bundle files, release metadata, archive names, and shared handoff metadata can be checked together before distribution.

Milestone 30T adds a reviewer-facing Release Review Package so the shared release handoff summary and artifact-integrity report can be previewed and exported together as one ZIP with README files and release notes.
Milestone 30U makes the reviewer ZIP self-describing by adding review-package-manifest.json plus matching preview/readiness text and packaged-manifest coverage.
Milestone 31A adds @acs/ai-core as a pure provider-contract layer for AI manifests, generation requests, proposal envelopes, provider registries, and request/proposal validation.
Milestone 31B extends @acs/ai-core with provider-independent generation plans and proposal review reports so later UI/API flows can present the same human-review-first AI workflow across providers.
Milestone 31C extends @acs/ai-core with portable generation session records that bundle request, plan, proposal, and review data into one stable AI handoff object.
Milestone 31D extends @acs/ai-core with proposal change summaries so later review UI can see what a structured proposal changes before acceptance.
Milestone 31E extends @acs/ai-core with proposal application plans so later review UI can show safe apply eligibility, blockers, and target sections before mutation.

Milestone 31F extends @acs/ai-core with portable review packages that bundle the session record, change summary, application plan, manifest metadata, and README guidance into one later export/import handoff object.
Milestone 31G extends @acs/ai-core with export-ready review bundles so later editor/API layers can emit concrete AI handoff files directly from one declared package format.
Milestone 31H extends @acs/ai-core with archive-ready review handoffs so later editor/API layers can emit one real AI review archive with declared entries and archive bytes.
Milestone 31I extends @acs/ai-core with shared AI handoff integrity reports so later editor/API layers can judge package, bundle, and archive readiness from one provider-agnostic object.
Milestone 31J extends @acs/ai-core with AI handoff import plans so later editor/API layers can decide whether a reviewed AI package is safe to ingest and what it preserves.

Milestone 31K extends @acs/ai-core with shared AI handoff import reports so later editor/API layers can summarize import readiness, blockers, preserved artifacts, and next-step guidance from one provider-agnostic reviewed handoff object.
milestone24Assets: Adventure presentation settings include splash asset id, starting music cue id, and intro text.
Classic visual manifests can contain editable 8x8 pixel sprite definitions with palette, pixels, usage, tags, and genre tags.
The editor exposes an Assets library panel with splash/music selectors, intro text, pixel sprite selector, palette selector, grid painting, and preview canvases.

The sample adventure includes seven starter genre packs: fantasy shrine, science-fiction data core, modern spy, superhero rooftops, science-fantasy gate, supernatural case file, and urban fantasy alley.

Starter libraries were expanded with ACS-inspired generic things, creatures, weapons, armor, keys, treasure, support NPCs, thief/slinker/fighter archetypes, shops, traps, stairs, signs, chests, bridges, and teleport pads.
milestone25Diagnostics: editor-core exposes createAuthoringDiagnostics for reusable authoring diagnostics
Test & Publish renders Authoring Diagnostics and Playtest Scenarios cards
tools/playtest-smoke.mjs validates and simulates start state, Oracle interaction, shrine reward, item grant, tile change, quest stage, and exit travel
milestone26LibraryPortability: packages/default-content exports builtInStarterLibrarySource metadata for application-owned starter libraries.
packages/default-content exports createLibraryCollections, createStarterLibrarySnapshot, createCustomLibraryExport, listStarterPacksByGenre, and listCustomLibraryObjectCounts.
Built-in starter libraries, adventure-local objects, and reusable custom library exports are documented as separate storage layers.
milestone27EditorInformationArchitecture: Selected-cell inspector derives tile, occupant, exit, and trigger summaries from the active AdventurePackage draft.
Display rename scans player-facing fields across adventure metadata, regions, maps, library categories, entities, items, tiles, quests, and dialogue.
Display rename deliberately avoids internal id and structured reference changes.
futurePublishingModes: Publishing should transform immutable releases into artifacts without mutating the original project draft or release snapshot.

forkableProject exports should include editable adventure source, authoring metadata, custom libraries, provenance, visual manifests, assets, remix/fork licensing metadata, and packaged handoff guidance for recipients.

Future forkable import should validate the artifact, show provenance and release metadata, preview included starter packs and custom libraries, import as a new editable project, and later support selective remix import of maps, quests, entities, items, tiles, assets, and library subsets.
standalonePlayable exports should include runtime-only package data, required assets, a play shell, distribution metadata, and no editor UI, draft history, source notes, or unused starter content.

packages/publishing now owns createForkableProjectExport, createStandaloneRuntimeExport, collectRuntimeAssets, pruneUnusedAuthoringData, attachStandaloneBundle, createForkableProjectPackageArchive, and validatePublishArtifact.
forkableProject artifacts now also carry a package manifest so editable handoff file lists can be validated and previewed before ZIP export.
Both export modes now also carry a shared ReleaseHandoffManifest and package RELEASE-HANDOFF.json so the release-level handoff story is visible in the editor and in shipped bundles.
The editor can now also export that shared release handoff manifest directly as a standalone JSON handoff document using the manifest-backed recommended filename.
Standalone export now carries a generated play-bundle manifest and the editor can package that manifest into a ZIP download.
Standalone export should start as a static web bundle and can later be wrapped for desktop without changing runtime-core.

Planned delivery modes for shipped standalone adventures are: manual static hosting, direct hosted-web sharing, a bundled local launcher that starts a small local web server automatically, and later desktop wrappers built on the same exported bundle.
currentPlanningCorrections: Exit is the underlying map-link record used by runtime travel. Portal is a designer-facing presentation/genre label for a tile, object, trigger, or exit that behaves like a magical/science-fiction passage. They can share traversal machinery while staying distinct in editor language.

Stacked tile effects are not yet complete as a rich first-class authoring model. The roadmap now calls for ordered action stacks on one cell: media/splash, message, give item, take item, stat boost, spawn/despawn NPC, tile change, quest update, sound cue, and portal travel.

All reusable gameplay values should become CRUD-enabled objects with categories, stable ids, duplicate warnings, and validation. Free-text lists are only temporary notes, not reusable game references.
Play mode should keep immediate state visible and add an open-at-need player profile panel for inventory, skills, traits, abilities, active effects, and quest-linked objects.

The legacy ACS timed-action mode should return as a supported play mode: movement drains the bar in larger chunks, idling trickles it away, and opening the player/toolbox panel is the intended pause affordance.

Default starter libraries should move out of sampleAdventure into reusable library data. Adventures may include custom additions or overrides, and designers should be able to import/export reusable custom library packs.

Editor workspaces should progressively disclose task-specific panels. Map Workspace should show only tile controls while painting tiles, only entity controls while placing or moving entities, and only exit/portal controls while editing travel links.
World structure should be added as optional authoring metadata over the existing graph model: regions organize maps, maps are playable spaces, and exits remain the runtime truth for travel.

The best implementation path is to keep WorldStructure, map authoring metadata, and exit presentation metadata in the content/domain layer while letting runtime continue to traverse exits rather than interpreting a strict hierarchy.

World Atlas and Map Workspace should teach the world model clearly through Structure, Connections, and Templates flows plus Travel Link terminology, without replacing the existing exit data model internally.

Targeted item usage should be implemented as designer-authored structured item-effect data over shared actor-action services, not as hardcoded player-only weapon/heal logic or one-off trigger scripts.

The safest placement for targeted item usage is split across shared runtime actor-action work, item authoring UI, player targeting UX, and later multiplayer reuse rather than forcing it into a single milestone.

The remaining completion checklist is durable project policy: explicit editor-completion, player-front-end, renderer-family-choice, and starter-library/graphics items should be tracked and reviewed at the start of every future milestone.

Editor UX theming should be treated like runtime rendering: the skin can change, but editing behavior should continue to flow through shared draft state, validation, project/release services, and editor-core mutation helpers.

Once multiple renderer families exist, designers should be able to choose the intended presentation target for an adventure such as classic 8-bit, enhanced 16-bit, HD 2D, or later 3D. That choice should live in presentation metadata and renderer manifests, not in separate engine forks.

Variable sprite scaling, render layers, and higher-resolution collision/rendering rules should be treated as optional higher-mode presentation capabilities; classic 8-bit mode should remain unchanged.
The safest placement for variable sprite scale is primarily Milestone 38 for renderer-family architecture plus Milestone 39 for higher-resolution asset authoring follow-through.

The WorldTree metaphor should shape top-level editor information architecture and onboarding language without replacing practical object-level labels like map, tile, entity, quest, trigger, dialogue, and asset.

A future legal-safe rebrand should move human-facing ACS / Adventure Construction Set naming toward WorldTree, but technical identifiers should migrate through compatibility shims and tested conversions instead of a blind global rename.

milestone36EditorCompletion: Replace sample-first editor boot with a project-first Adventure Hub that supports Create New Adventure, Open Existing Adventure, and explicit sample opening.
Apply the WorldTree information-architecture spine to editor navigation and workflow framing: Seed Creator, Root Engine, Branch Builder, Leaf Layer, Forest Hub, and Growth Mode.
Finish universal CRUD for reusable object types: tiles, entities, items, dialogue, quests, objectives, rewards, media cues, sound cues, starter packs, categories, and templates.
Finish progressive-disclosure behavior consistently across all editor workspaces.
Separate editor presentation from editor behavior so future UX skins can reuse the same draft state, validation flows, project/release actions, and editor-core mutation helpers.
Add reusable help-icon tooltip patterns for terminology, structure, trigger logic, publishing modes, and other non-obvious fields.
Route validation issues contextually so high-level project health stays in Adventure Setup while actionable library, map, travel, and logic issues appear in their relevant workspaces.
Finish stacked tile/action authoring for classic ACS-style multi-effect cells.

Refactor World Atlas into a cleaner left-to-right drilldown with no ambiguous preselection, no always-on structure chart, clear selected-map versus create-map separation, and a selectable fill-tile dropdown.
Clean up user-facing map terminology around World, Zone, and Local Scene while keeping internal identifiers stable until a dedicated schema rename is justified.

Add optional world-structure authoring metadata in the existing seams: AdventurePackage.worldStructure, map authoring metadata for scale/role/region-entry/atlas position, and exit presentation metadata for connection type, atlas visibility, label, and optional lock hints.

Implement world-structure normalization conservatively in packages/content-schema so old adventures load, safe defaults can be inferred, fake exits are never invented, and world-structure metadata stays authoring-only rather than runtime-controlling.

Add pure editor-core world-topology operations for overworld assignment, map role/scale assignment, region-entry marking, atlas positioning, travel-link creation, return-link creation, and travel-link updates/removal.

Extend diagnostics with world-topology checks such as missing overworld, region without entry map, invalid exit target, duplicate travel links, orphaned maps, and unreachable maps, routed to World Atlas, Map Workspace, and Test & Publish.
Upgrade World Atlas into explicit Structure, Connections, and Templates flows that teach: regions organize maps, maps are playable spaces, and Travel Links connect maps.

In World Atlas, add a primary-overworld selector, region-entry management, local-map organization, graph visibility, and classic-world template generation without turning the graph into a strict hierarchy.

In Map Workspace, rename visible exit authoring language to Travel Link while preserving the underlying exit data model, and add Travel Link inspector actions such as Edit, Create Return Link, and Open Target Map.

Add a Classic Overworld template generator that can create an overworld map, regions, entry maps, starter local maps, and optional return/internal travel links without introducing renderer-specific logic.
Finish starter/custom library import, export, copy/fork, and duplicate-warning flows.
Add full item target/effect authoring in Libraries so designers can define self/entity/tile targeting, structured item effects, target restrictions, and validation without scripting.

Add guided forkable-project import with validation, provenance/release preview, import-as-new-project flow, and later selective remix import for maps, quests, entities, items, tiles, assets, and library subsets.
Finish richer atlas/map dependency editing and map/region/world drilldown.
milestone37PlayerFrontEnd: Add the legacy ACS-style timed-action mode with a movement bar that drains faster on movement, trickles down while idle, and pauses only when the player profile/toolbox panel is open.
Add a meaningful player profile panel for inventory, equipped items, skills, traits, powers, active effects, and quest-linked objects.
Finish player-facing visibility for owned objects, status changes, and richer state inspection.
Finish classic/debug dialogue and message presentation so conversations, item changes, cues, and quest updates coexist cleanly.
Add scrollable dialogue history, response-choice presentation, and extensible player-input surfaces so the front end is ready for future bounded AI/NPC conversations.
Keep turn-based and timed-action play as explicit runtime modes so classic pacing remains available after player-front-end expansion.
Prepare the dialogue front end for future bounded dynamic NPC conversation with history, response choices, and extensible input surfaces.

Add optional runtime travel metadata support while keeping exits as the runtime truth: locked/gated travel through required flags, locked-travel messages, and player-facing travel events that can surface connection type or label.
Add targeted-item-use player UX with self-use shortcuts, valid-target highlighting, tile cursors, cancel flows, and clear feedback in both classic and future skinned shells.
milestone38RendererChoice: Add multi-renderer presentation families such as classic 8-bit, enhanced 16-bit, HD 2D, and future 3D.
Let new-adventure creation begin with a presentation-family choice so designers can intentionally target retro, richer 2D, or later higher-end presentation styles.
Let designers choose the intended visual target for a new adventure.

Add the variable-sprite-scale presentation foundation for higher-resolution modes: optional entity/tile visual scales, render layers, HD sprite definitions, and renderer-specific scale handling while keeping classic 8-bit mode unchanged.
Add shared higher-resolution runtime support such as optional pixel-accurate collision, Euclidean missile-distance/path logic, and z-layer draw ordering only where those renderer families need it.
Keep renderer choice in presentation metadata and renderer/asset manifests, not in separate engine forks.
Support previewing the same adventure content across multiple renderer targets.
milestone40WorldTreeRebrandMigration: Replace human-facing ACS / Adventure Construction Set branding with WorldTree branding across UI text, docs, exports, and packaged handoff materials.

Audit and migrate technical identifiers such as package scopes, local-storage keys, manifest names, app titles, and artifact labels through explicit compatibility shims rather than risky global renames.
Add migration coverage so existing drafts, saves, releases, imports, and exports continue to load while the public-facing name changes.
milestone39StarterLibrariesAndGraphics: Complete legacy ACS starter-library import/recreation, excluding Land of Adventuria-specific content.
Rename or replace any non-public-domain starter-pack asset names that could create avoidable copyright or trademark confusion.
Add western, mythology, medieval, and steampunk starter packs with organized entities, terrain, items, structures, hazards, and support objects.

Add higher-resolution asset authoring follow-through for renderer families: HD sprite records, scale-aware previews, optional collision-mask authoring, and validation that higher-resolution assets match intended scales and layers.
Finish graphics coverage for starter entities, terrain, items, structures, hazards, and support objects.
Make starter visuals editable anywhere the corresponding visual object is edited.
Move starter packs fully into reusable default-content ownership and support genre-pack selection when beginning a new adventure.

milestone28PresentationMedia: Classic ACS play mode supports Compact, Large, and Extra Large renderer-local scaling through CanvasGameRenderer classicScale options.
AdventurePackage includes mediaCues and soundCues collections.
Trigger actions include playMedia and playSound; runtime-core emits mediaCuePlayed and soundCuePlayed events.
The editor no-code action builder can add Play Media Cue and Play Sound Cue actions.
Validation checks cue asset references and trigger action cue references.
qualityGates: Milestone 29E adds docs/tutorial-acceptance.json and extends docs validation so the Relay Station tutorial must retain required step titles, text cues, unique screenshots, and PDF/image outputs.

runtimeFlows.name: Move command
steps: Browser key handler maps an arrow/WASD key to a runtime command.
Game engine dispatches command to GameSession.
Movement helpers validate target map cell, blocked tiles, entities, exits, and triggers.
State is updated if movement is valid.
Enemy turn system advances according to authored turn pacing and behavior profiles.
Runtime events are emitted.
Browser renders the new state through the selected visual mode.

runtimeFlows.name: Inspect or interact command
steps: Browser key handler sends inspect/interact command to runtime-core.
Runtime resolves adjacent or current interactable target.
Dialogue, trigger, item gate, quest stage, teleport, reward, or tile-change effects are executed as structured actions.
State and event log update.
Classic mode writes messages to the bottom band; debug mode may expose richer debug UI.

runtimeFlows.name: Pixel sprite edit
steps: Designer opens Libraries > Assets.
Editor UI selects a ClassicPixelSpriteDefinition from the active classic visual manifest.
Designer selects a palette index and paints a grid cell.
editor-core setClassicPixelSpritePixel updates the sprite pixel array by stable sprite id.
Editor rerenders the grid, small preview, and large preview immediately.

editorFlows.name: Libraries drilldown
steps: Choose Library Type such as Entities, Items, Tiles, Quests, Dialogue, or Assets.
Choose a category/class for that type.
Choose a concrete object.
Edit details in the full-width details panel below the drilldown columns.
Objects with graphics should eventually expose graphics assignment and editing directly from this details surface.

editorFlows.name: Map workspace
steps: Select a map without leaving the workspace.
Choose a tile brush and paint multiple cells.
Place or move entity instances that reference entity definitions.
Optionally give placed entity instances friendly display names and simple behavior overrides.
Attach exits and trigger markers to selected cells.
Playtest the selected map and validate references.

milestone24Audit.item: Adventure presentation settings for splash screen, music cue, and intro text
Manifest-backed classic pixel sprites with palette and pixel grid
Assets editor panel with splash/music selectors and compact 8x8 pixel painting grid
Stocked starter genre packs for fantasy, sci-fi, modern spy, superhero, science fantasy, supernatural, and urban fantasy
Genre-specific groups for reusable tiles, entities, items, skills, traits, spells, and assets
Full object-specific graphics editing for every visual object type
milestone24Audit.status: planned, not part of original Milestone 24 completion

milestone24Audit.item: Library UI parent-child drilldown with full-width detail editor below
milestone24Audit.status: partially scaffolded after Milestone 24; planned for Milestone 27 completion

aiAgnosticProviderLayer.status: foundation implemented through Milestone 31K; adapter and UI work still planned
requirements: Provider interface separate from domain/runtime/editor core.
Adapters for any LLM or agent API that can return structured proposals.
Schema validation, reference checks, duplicate warnings, provenance, and human review before mutation.
Use normal AdventurePackage objects as output.
Support full adventure drafting, partial scene expansion, and gap filling.
Respect designer source notes, genre constraints, starter packs, and validation.

aiNpcBrain.status: planned for optional AI runtime phase
aiNpcBrain.description: Designer-authored context that guides an optional AI provider when speaking or proposing behavior for an NPC.
guardrails: AI text may enrich dialogue, but game-state changes must be validated actions.
Deterministic behavior remains default and offline-safe.
Cost, cooldown, safety, and testability controls are mandatory.

aiNpcEnvironmentInteraction.status: planned for optional AI runtime phase, with actor-action readiness work before AI execution
currentReadiness: Player actions are structured commands, but command handling is currently player-centered.
EnemyTurnSystem moves enemies but does not use player-equivalent item, trigger, portal, or support actions.
TriggerSystem does not yet receive actor context, so permissions and effects cannot distinguish player versus NPC actor.
requiredCapabilities: ActorCommand or RuntimeActionProposal model shared by player, deterministic NPCs, and AI NPCs.
Actor identity in runtime validation so commands apply to the correct entity position, inventory, faction, and map.
Targeted item-use proposals that can address self, other actors, and tiles through shared validated action data.
Designer-authored capability profiles for support, informational, random, rival, and antagonist NPCs.
Explicit allow/deny permissions for items, triggers, portals/exits, maps, object classes, and action categories.
Actor-aware trigger context and actor inventories/equipment.
Validated action proposals only; AI must not directly mutate state.

sharedActorActions.status: readiness evaluator implemented, full runtime execution refactor planned
sharedActorActions.principle: Players and AI/NPC actors should use the same validated runtime action functions; individual items, exits, triggers, maps, and action categories can still be player-only, NPC-only, blocked, all-actor, or explicit allow-list.
implementedCoverage: enemy item-use denial through object policy
support NPC exit readiness through capability profile
informational NPC item-use blocking through action and profile policy
remainingWork: designer-authored targeted item effects with target restrictions and validation
shared actor command services for movement, inspect, interact, item use, trigger activation, and exit traversal
deterministic runtime events for rejected NPC/AI action proposals

preMilestoneBuildChecklist: Read docs/roadmap.html for milestone order, accepted future capabilities, and editor-area planning.
Read docs/system-reference.md for architecture, implementation explanations, known gaps, and future constraints.
Read docs/llm-project-context.json for compact AI-readable project structure, data model, flows, future planning, and current milestone status.
Read docs/codex-chat-log.json for collaboration history, user preferences, milestone decisions, and corrections.
Read docs/user-guide.md for current user-facing tutorial expectations and screenshot requirements.
Read docs/quality/engineering-quality.md for complexity, SOLID, organization, and cleanup expectations.
afterEachMilestone: Update roadmap.html plus the other related durable planning/reference documents whenever planning or implementation changes are accepted.
Update User Guide tutorial with creative feature exercises and current screenshots.
Update System Reference with architecture, sequence diagrams, implementation details, and screenshots.
Regenerate PDFs when human-facing guide/reference documents change.
Ensure screenshots in PDFs are current and reflect the exact task or purpose they illustrate.
Ensure the User Guide tutorial shows each step with its own meaningful screenshot or focused cropped screenshot.
Update this LLM-readable project context with new architecture, data, flows, status, and known gaps.

multiplayerArchitecture.recommendedFoundation: Backend-authoritative multiplayer sessions where clients send serializable actor commands, the server validates through portable runtime-core, advances canonical state, and broadcasts snapshots or event deltas.
why: Shared sessions need one source of truth for turn order, triggers, inventory, NPC actions, saves, and replay/persistence.
runtime-core can remain usable in the browser for solo play and on the backend for multiplayer if it stays DOM-free and deterministic.
Peer-to-peer may be explored later for private/friendly modes, but it is not the first foundation because cheating, conflict resolution, host migration, and reconnection are harder.
requiredPreparation: Player and NPC actions converge on actor-capable action services.
Targeted item usage stays on those shared actor-capable action services so player-to-player and player-to-NPC item use reuse the same validation path.
Persistence can store authoritative session snapshots and replay/audit events.
Diagnostics can simulate multi-actor scenarios and permission problems.

targetedItemUsage.principle: Targeted item usage should be designer-authored structured effect data over shared actor-action services, not hardcoded player-only weapon/heal logic.
phasedPlacement: Milestone 33 runtime and shared actor-action foundation
Milestone 36 item target/effect authoring UI and validation
requirements: Target self, entity, tile, and later multiplayer actor interactions through serializable target data.
Keep item effects structured and validatable, with target restrictions, optional cue feedback, and backward-compatible trigger fallback when needed.
Route execution through shared actor-owned state, actor-aware triggers, and normal runtime events.
Avoid one-off trigger-script patterns for common weapon, heal, buff, transfer, or tile-change item behaviors.

variableSpriteScale.principle: Variable sprite scale, render layers, and optional pixel-accurate higher-mode collision should prepare richer renderer families without changing classic 8-bit gameplay behavior.
requirements: Keep visual scale optional on entities, tiles, and higher-resolution visual-manifest records.
Allow render layers and scale-aware rendering only in the renderer families that need them.
Use optional higher-mode collision and missile-distance logic without replacing the simpler classic-mode path.
Keep editor authoring for scale, layers, and collision masks scoped to higher-resolution asset workflows rather than classic-only flows.

mobilePlayMode.principle: Phone-sized devices should be play-only, not edit/create devices.
requirements: Use screen-size and capability gates to hide or block editor/create workflows on phones.
Provide touch controls that dispatch the same movement, inspect, interact, continue-dialogue, and profile commands as keyboard play.
Keep mobile as a presentation/input adapter over the same runtime state and command model.
Support readable scaling, message-band visibility, objective/state display, and a profile/inventory drawer.
Standalone playable publishing should provide a direct play URL or installable shell without editor source controls.
mobilePlayMode.openDecision: Larger tablet editing can be evaluated later, but phone editing remains intentionally out of scope.

contextualVisualEditors.principle: Any object with a visual binding should expose graphical editing in that object detail panel, not only in a generic Assets panel.

milestone25Audit.item: Reusable authoring diagnostics model and builder
Visible diagnostics and scenario prompts in Test & Publish

milestone25Audit.status: complete for Markdown/HTML; PDF regeneration depends on an available local renderer
